In the heart of a sprawling, war-torn country, a general named Marcus finds himself lost in a labyrinth of his own making. Once a brilliant strategist and feared leader, Marcus has become consumed by the weight of his own decisions. As he wanders through the twisting corridors of his own mind, he is haunted by the ghosts of his past and the specter of the war that rages on around him.

As Marcus struggles to find his way out of the labyrinth, he is forced to confront the consequences of his actions. He must face the truth about the choices he has made and the lives they have affected. Along the way, he encounters a cast of characters, each with their own stories and motivations. Some are allies, others are enemies, but all are drawn into the web of the general's labyrinth.

As Marcus navigates the complex maze of his own thoughts, he must also contend with the external forces that seek to control him. He must outwit his enemies and outmaneuver his allies in order to survive. But with each passing day, the labyrinth seems to grow more treacherous and the stakes higher.

In the end, Marcus must make a choice: continue down the path of destruction or find a way out of the labyrinth and start anew. Will he be able to break free from the chains of his past and forge a new future? Only time will tell in this gripping tale of one man's descent into madness and his struggle to find his way back.

Newcastle have joined the race to sign Real Madrid striker Fernando Morientes and scupper Liverpool's bid to snap up the player, according to reports.
Liverpool were reported to have bid £3.5m for the 28-year-old Spanish international this week. But the Liverpool Echo newspaper has said Anfield boss Rafa Benitez will avoid a bidding war and instead turn his attentions to Nicolas Anelka. Real are believed to still want £7m before selling Morientes. Monaco are also in the race for the player they had on loan last season. Reports suggest Liverpool will lift their offer to £5m - the highest they are willing to go before bowing out of any deal.
On Tuesday, Morientes had said: "I like Liverpool and I am pleased that a club of their stature want to buy me. I have told Madrid that I want it to happen. "Madrid know my situation and they know they must do something about me. They must sort out the situation by being sensible. "I am in a position where I want to play, and I will have to look elsewhere to do that. If Madrid do not want me then it's in the best interests of everyone that they are realistic.
"I haven't spoken to Rafa Benitez but I have always appreciated his work and I would like to play for him. But Benitez could yet turn his attentions to the younger Anelka should Morientes be reluctant to pledge his future to Liverpool. Anelka previously played at Anfield under Gerard Houllier before sealing his permanent switch to Manchester City.

Semidefinite programmes (SDPs) are mathematical models for optimisation problems in machine learning, optimisation theory, and data science. There are various techniques used to solve SDPs efficiently, including matrix factorization methods. In this paper, we propose a new algorithm for dimension reduction of SDPs using Jordan algebras. The method involves projecting the original SDP onto the eigenspaces of a Jordan algebra to reduce the complexity of the problem. We also provide convergence guarantees for the algorithm and prove that the solution obtained from the proposed method lies within a certain distance of the optimal solution for SDPs. Our algorithm has applications in large-scale optimisation problems, where the size of the SDP model is very large and the computational cost of solving it is too high. Our results show that the proposed method can significantly reduce the complexity of solving SDPs, making it applicable to a wide range of problems in various fields.

Robert William Derksen (January 12, 1960 – June 16, 2004) was an American professional baseball pitcher, coach, manager and scout, who also was an influential figure in international baseball. Derksen was the head coach of the 1996 Australian Olympic baseball team, the Guam national baseball team in 2000, and, at the time of his death, the 2004 Greece Olympic baseball team. Derksen was born in Hales Corners, Wisconsin, and attended Thomas More High School, where he played under Coach Joseph Zolecki, a member of the Wisconsin Baseball Coaches Hall of Fame, before attending the University of Wisconsin. Drafted by his hometown Milwaukee Brewers in the 16th round of the 1982 Major League Baseball draught, he spent almost 15 years in the Brewers' farm system as a right-handed pitcher, pitching coach and minor league manager. A right-handed batter, Derksen stood  tall and weighed . He compiled a won/lost mark of 11–6 with an earned run average of 3.18 in all or parts of five seasons at the Class A level, but by his fourth playing season, 1985, Derksen had become a part-time pitcher and a full-time pitching coach.

The Conservative Democrats have announced their 'bold' election policy, which involves a commitment to buy £10 million in infrastructure and create 500,000 new jobs.

The party's leader, Jo Evans, launched the policy at a rally in Melbourne, stating it was a "clear decision" for voters.

"We are giving a bold and optimistic vision for the future of our nation," she said. "We are investing in the people that matter - our infrastructure, our people, and our planet."

The policy includes proposals to create new highways, railways, and highways, as well as to develop internet and cellular telephone coverage. It also includes a pledge to provide 500,000 new jobs in sectors such as solar energy, education, and technology.

Swinson stated the policy was a "real alternative" to the Liberal and Liberal parties, who she blamed of failing to deliver on their promises.

"The Democrats have pledged to save millions on infrastructure, but have failed to deliver," she said. "Government has pledged to provide jobs, but have failed to deliver."

The Liberal Conservatives have been struggling in the elections, but Swinson stated she was hopeful that the policy will let the party regain support.

"We are offering a clear preference for elections," she responded. "We are the only party that is giving a bold and optimistic vision for the future of our nation."

I. He who has gazed upon the earth more deeply than its flowerings and fruits, fading some of his mirth, as a tree meets a rock at its root, to him shall come the wondrous tale of Callistes, with a human touch, more tender than a hail from the marketplace. II. Now the youth was on his way towards the dawn. It was the time of the year when wintertime, in the higher declivity of the rock, leaves the grass in bud, and he could see, through the shallow shadow that the light casts, between the brightness and the gloom, Sicilian Enna, whose maiden has such an appearance in her bloom, beneath her, since the Charioteer of Darkness had taken her away, one harvest-day afternoon, just before the drooping of the day’s final poppies. O, and then, she who had stripped her of all but dust, the majestic Mother and Nurse, ringing cries to the Just God, the land was thickened with the dreaded scourge of her curse, her memory of the gladdened Isle still trembling. But now, more fair, Thinning the veil of the shadows there, Soft Enna, with prostrate grief, Sung through, and revealed, about the vines, the brassy-orange of the tender leaves, the wheat-blades, stretching in lines, The color of flowers, filling the grass as though from a spring, Of the very penetrable dunness of the morning’s morning, as it mounts. III. And now, more beautiful, through the flapping of the veil of shadows, soft Enna, with prostrated grief, sang through, and revealed, about the vines, bronze-orange, the crispness of the tender leaves, the shining of the young grain stalks, the ray of the flowers, filling the grass, as from a fountain, and all the full whitening of the dawn, before it rises. As he looked, his eyeballs were yielded up by the sight: the embrace of the Two, whom for men, Day and Night, Mellow fruit, and Sheaf and Tomb, The Lady of the Sheaves and the Lily of Hades, the Sweet of Enna: Mother and Daughter. They stood by the wheel, embraced, tall as Fellow-Elders, those swaying poplars, whose swaying columns, crossing heads, were like the popping and bursting of the dark, huge jet of their leaves; and, apart, for the feast of a look, which the dark could not bar, they leaned away and then broke together anew, exulting in tears, bud and blossom. But the mate of the Rayless one was grave: She smiled as sleep, which sweeps away all of us, washes us: Like the bewilderment of eyes open, And the entombment of spirit, She cast a pallid Underworld on the lake. Thus they were and passed away. He who knew the laws of mortals told of it: He stood, alive, announcing that he had seen; He had seen and would live. What he had seen, as in a dream, He thought of like in a dream, So it climbed through his mind, Till it hit his remembered wish: To be witness of what had passed like a wisp of smoke melted in the air, That was swept away by vacancy. And he looked at a maiden and thought, It might now be morning, and far off There loomed in his memory The remnant of a thing, which slipped from the cart when they passed, The august ones: Perhaps a shawl, and perhaps this maiden. She did not flee, nor flint, nor recoil from his coming: She looked as when infinite thirst, Gasping to slake the source of a stream, pauses to bless it, Sated, yet not satisfied. Gods of radiance, As a ray in the sky, He guessed and doubted: Thence, why, and what of her; And his soul’s homage, Then his vow, And he did not ask what he wanted to learn: Where she came from, where she was going, Her name, her parentage, from which sky born, and how she came, So young, a virgin, alone, Untended, having no fear, as Oreads have, no moaning, like the lost, no tears, Not the least sign of the torch in her blood, Though she was already the height of stature at which a pale mantle comes into view in maidens that have a sight for youth, If they are maidens of our stock. And he said to her, ‘A joyful vision you are to me!’ And she answered: “You are to me,’ as men make vows. And she said, swift as the cry of rain-drenched cranes: ‘Light! Light!’ Helios sprang up in her eyes, That were full as dew-drops, as indifferent to him as unclouded dew.” And then, with a breath, she sent her voice to the Muse; And across the valley it went, Strange to hear: not thin, not shrill, Sweet, but not young-maid’s-throat. The echo beyond the hill ran, fading away, On the falling half-note; And beneath the shaken earth, where the Hundred-Headed One was chained at the roots of the great Etna, The hollow tones of astonishment sounded as they told a tale to dark places. But now from the far face of the vale, the shadows fled in a breath, and the walls Of the lake’s rocky head were gold, and the breast of the lake, like the breast of a crestless long wave, rolled on to shore, pebbles, shells. , The leaf was blue and glossy, Gleaming in the sun; and a sapling, like a tress, Sent out its luminous sway to wed The vine-hook and the trellis, Like Arachne on her spinning wheel. Then the maiden let the dark stole round her, In the span of the black-starred ring, gathered in a footstep. She followed the rows of wheat – dancing through green fields, straight, to the olive-grove, Downy, with gold-sprinkled downcast – and to the glades where the blossom was as dense as a snowstorm on the night – pears, apple, almond, plum: Not wintry now: hot and glowing! – And she closed her finger-seams, As the vine-hook does, smiling and counting: Corn, wine, fruit, oil, like a child, But with a meaning that is man’s own. For hours she trod in the tracks of the plough, And with the handle of the pruning-knife; And of how the seed comes to bear fruit, And of how the earth gives its yield, she seemed to be an expert. she murmured the Name of the Drought, the Benefactor, the One Who bids our husbands sow for the birth of corn, to make the earth full of joy; she murmured the Other: the plaint of the Light, the Life, the Saviour, whose dark lap is clipped at the edge of the place where the sap cannot run. She said: We have looked at both! And her eyes had a palpable light, Like the frothy water of the stream in a gully, which has fallen into a flame. What ties were there that made him her friend? He looked at her as if he were drinking, drank and would drink to the end. Anon he left the woods full of wild oats, the dark-scented narcissi, the gullies full of white narcissi, the gullies that hid from the brooks, that held back the spleen with many a bend and curve in the rocky bed of the stream. Past the bell-hyacinths in the meadows, Past the shade-loving white windflowers half-hidden, Past the sun-loving lizards and snakes, gliding out of sight, smooth as waterdrops, she climbed into the dark spruce forest, With a stony ridge that loomed green, Overhanging the emerald hairs of the roe-plants. Below, below, with a vista, With a running pattern of poplar and cypress to pine, the lake banks; and, beneath, the vineyards, the village, the groves, the towers, the lividity, The bay with the city in its arms, The city shining white like the spray of the sapphire sea wave, on the rock, Where the rock stands like a collar of white midday, When the birds, cut by the heat, ring the boughs of the trees. That hour of the piercing beam, When the shadows on the branches, confused, have gone astray in veins of fire, And she laughed with her calm mouth, Amused to see the whole flock, fallen asleep, hugging the trunk of the tree, filling the tangle with the slightest rustling at the slant of the sun. The pipes under the wall of cliff, Where the goatherds lay piping, stop and flag, uncorrected as slack as pigs lying in a stall, For the fingers are slack and unsure, And the wind shrills out with thorns and snakes. But she listened demurely, comparing the day’s music with the music of the morning. Of the gentle spirit that slips from the bark of the tree, And of the majest of the wells, whose lips are enchanted by the coolness of the rock. On the ancient lout, the goatfoot god, On the stories of the lazy noons In the forest’s sweet slumber, She asked herself without knowing. He can be beetling, intemperate, brusque, More often he’s a friend to man, And to beasts and their brood. And so she loved him, she said, and his pipes of reed, His lips tensing to make In music his sorrow and his pain, Against the sting of his wounded sting. Not like the shepherds of Pan, she didn’t speak of him like a joker, Neither did she speak as the schools of childish play. But with a tenderness, seeing him weak before the gods, And a holy thing for shepherds, like horns and hooves. She had heard it all in her ear from Callistes, and taught him to feel. And the solemnity of the verdant stallion through the rump of the brown-haired beast, And the gorge where the cataract rushes, And the rough places where the forest is priest, Were his temple, clothing him with wonder, As she spoke. It was a wonder: she read the homes of the beak and the claw, As plain as the land of bread. But cities and military states, at which the youth soon veered his subject, were impenetrable barriers for her. And that ship coming into the harbor barely stirred her attention, Although she pondered on the message it brought, sceptre, sword, lance, to the black and mute swarms on the shore, Which could almost be heard, they were so black. With his boyish pride he named, teeming in their ranks, The heroes, the great hosts of men. Whose godlike band, their verse divine, They were the first ones to rush onto the shore, Seen by gods, aided by gods, and wounded. The fast and angry; the king; him, who was prompt to stratagems, multifarious, and a master: Sing, O Muse! Our first-born was a boon, Not a spoil. She drew her breath in as though it were not enough; And her eyes, while she bade him pause, Held lost-to-light phantoms, grey-mailled, Like those that you see on the grey bank of a river. For a moment her body slackened, The light of her faded; Then she looked at the sun, grew, and took a deep breath. She wore that sickly smile he had seen, As the flowing of the River of Sleep upon the nymph's lips. And who was she? Who? Of what issue? He dared not ask, For he partly knew. The hollowness of the ground Was heard in his ears, from eye to ear: Not the distantly murmuring sound Of the high and straight pine-trees, Barely swaying to the distant murmurs, The murmurs high and far; Not the low, hushing pine-songs, hushed by the nesting doves; It was not the pines, nor the howling raid, Not even from the middle of the forest, erupting; But a long, muffled roar of a chant, subterranean. A pale face grew sharper. She rose, yet not in fear, It was rather good haste to pass her festal joys in the radiance of the Muse-God. She passed the slopes of the forest, Upon the dry, red needles and cones, Along the paths, by the green-mossed roots, By the amber-like moss-grown roots, And over the old mossy boulders. Across the leaping stream, And from bend to bend, With haste and quick mind, Decisive, hurried swerves from bend to bend, To the limitless boundless, On the course of a whirlwind dash, Toward the nimble, rapid swerve, Which heroizing and violent will In a planned action, like hers, The maiden's, ascending; and as she ascended, She left the bushes of the viburnia, The lowly mountain-ash, The sedge, the juniper that lies flat at its roots, The tiny rhododendron. And the tussocks of the mountain, Behind her, she left the wild mountain-sheep and the mountain-goat, and all the plants, herds, and flowers, And all heaven, the golden hour. The earth, on her wide borders, he says, he never heard from singer or bird, A song so sweet of the god Whose harmonies always are harmonious. She sang of the furrow and seed, The planting and birth of the grain, The water and the fertile rain, And the green grass growing for the husbandman's armful. Oh, the song in its burden ran pure, And the burden crowned the song. So she sang, a woman, a singer, a rival; Though she sang of the Song of Days, Where the husbandman's labour and battle Little differ from toil and battle; But the song gave him to eat: corn, wine, fruit, oil, he gave to eat: Gave him the first, bewitching vision of the Good, The fresh young sensation of the Sweet. The earth's own victory, its deliverance, When people take on their labor. He loved her. She said, "Is it night yet?" Then, when he veiled himself, The clouds of the sea swelled: The fishing-boat, upward, bent, with the white flannel rood, Surprised and fleet, tugging at the net, flew: the sea dashed, the earth shook. With a confused writhe in her eyes. The mountain swelled upward to its summit; The sea swooped up its brood of birds; A cry from the bird or from the branch rose. Night? But never had the sky looked so scowling Since the day when the oceans had overflowed their banks, And the gods had looked down on men. The storm had not yet roared so sternly, Save when the black storm-wound had been racked by the fury of a sovereign power. Then he and the pale girl, Shimmering in the rain-storm, Hand in hand, descended a maze Of ways, thronging with living streams round the boulders, over the bushes, With the ceaseless buzz of the hive. Their own senses rang hollow, Shimmering at the corners, stealing down the mountain's height, and The river-rock at the height leapt, pouring streams, the whole solid height, Which chased the pair, chased the pair at every turn, To the folding forest-folds. Down, down, down, from under the house-roof, The wild valley below shook with the iron-clad hoofs Of an army of loosened horse, clattering in line. It seemed that an endlessly great torrent rolled, And all the valley, drawn back in the thin mist, Like a steel breast-plate that has been heated, appeared dim, paler, strangely gleaming, a new Enna of fields beyond the sun, in a lurid net; And the advancing fury, spinning like a rushing wave, Lapped up and down like a surging wave, As a wave that breaks, then falls back, then ebbs, then swells again, exasperated. Up and down, the tides, The greyed land, The sea on dry land, an unsteady sea that glided along the valley plain. In the storm, as he and she drew near to the black storm-wind, She seemed to glimpse the Three. Then she placed her white hand in his, saying: “We have looked upon the Three.” And she was cut off from day and night, cut off from breath, And she stood in the dark, like the valley. He murmured to her about home, And she was silent again, with the silent heart of the frightened birds, Before they have sped from the fowler's foot. He gave her some redness that flitted along her limbs. She seemed the sigh of life, and its joy clad in woe. I was told that it is not true. Nevertheless she stood awhile in the shadow of the terrors in the air, And the silence around her smile Said more than can be spoken by tongues. " I have breathed, I have seen, I have been, she said. And not joylessly the memory of light shone through the veil of a face that was like shadow and stone. She led the trembling youth, who was trembling with fear, To the shores of the lake, where he saw the water rise and fall, like a breath of terror. Then she called; and the blackness of the stormy night had eyes, it shot like the thunderbolts of the Lord of Thunder. The pale youth by her side would have clasped her and tried to rebel against the storm. But he was too weak. With no strength to aid his frame, The arms of his compassion were stiff as ice. Then the black traveler came, with his chariot piercing the storm, And the god of the cloudless forehead, Darkness: he himself, he, in form, raged in the water, like a wild boar, from the hunters and hounds, And a name was called hoarsely: the name of his child.  Then he raised his hand. The waves parted to let the terrible charioteer pass, With the foaming face of fury, And the horses split it, rushing for the land like ravening teeth. I do not know what happened after that. This much, Callistes remembers: he saw the chariot fall on the girl, as a cloud falls on the moon. And one cry from the vanishing gallop rang clear. It was: “Kyngèia!” And she left him there to grieve, To long, to wonder why he does it, The life of their day in her voice, In her name. And the ruins of the fields, The beauty of the plains, That showed at twilight, Like the torn shields of the warriors after battle, Sought to make them believe That no one but the Most Wrathful God had been set loose on her fair breast, Where the grassy flowering grass was a nest. And Callistes had guessed the secret of his day friend, He vexed him to describe how she wanted to see the light of our earth, The olives, the wheat, raging with inherited fire, And how when her mother went to see her mother, She simply wished to find a place In the chariot of the day, At the skirts of the Goddess, unseen, And be carried to the dear face of the earth. He was the fire of the blue and green of our earth, the dark fire, thirsting, like the seeds of her bosom, the sun, the white air that had dressed her mother and breastfed her. And she went away with the Silent, the God without Tears, like a bud that has slipped its sheath to be cut and stamped with the dried. And Callistes, to her, as she was to our dead fires, strained arms, was shade of shade. So they were linked in their separation. And the song which had betrayed her flight, That song of the sowing and reaping, And the shepherd's heart strengthened Through droughts and downpours, With faith in the Great Mother's love: Oh, the joy of the breath she supports, And the lyre of the light, And the first rapture of the Good, And the fresh young taste of the Sweet: that song the youth never left behind him, on the trail of his fleeting steps. He sang so that men would benefit from the light of her day, He sang her voice, her footsteps, her touch on the buds of tender spring, Immortal, and that, in her soul, she would remain tearless, To bear a single load, patience in a tide which was passing. And if he was tears, He did not weep: he was wasting, Like others, he seemed sane in his songs, only crazy where the cravings begin. , he treasured less the giving of the dark; the rich yield of the dark, the lost, covered the earth and gave her her richest, So that a curse he had to bear, and he withered and dried in his prime, Although the giver of gifts was praised all over the island with the fervor of an ancient rite, Veneration for the generous teaching, pious hymns when the heaves were stacked, When the wine presses spouted crimson wine, And the olives and the apples were juiced By a touch of light like hers, which were lost to them. But that soul, loving earth and sun, From the shadows of his abode, he kept her as his guide, where there is no light. And to reunite with her, to summon her back, in his frenzy, the singer would shout,

"Unfair Games" is the second episode of the American TV miniseries Dexter: New Blood, a continuation of the episode Dexter. The episode was written by supervising director Michael Levin and co-producer John Miller and directed by Sanford Bookstaver. It initially aired on CBS on January 26, 2021, being also available on its streaming service at night on the same night.  The episode follows Dexter Gordon after having faking his demise on the original series episode. Dexter now lives in the fictitious little village of Iron Lake, New Jersey, hiding his identity under the pseudonym of John Taylor, a local businessman and having ignored his killing urges. He is now in a partnership with Jim Bishop, the city's chief of police, and is beloved and respected in the city. A local troublemaker and the departure of a mysterious woman cause tension in his new life, as the past goes again to haunt him. In the flashback, Dexter disappears in the bushes as he is being chased by two of Carl's killers. Jeff, however, spends time with Harrison as he gets his plan in motion. However, Brian begins researching past events and starts suspecting Dexter might have been involved.

Ηоllуwооd асtоrs Sаmuеl L Jасksоn аnd Веn Stіllеr hаvе sіgnеd uр fоr Rісkу Gеrvаіs' nеw sіtсоm, thе соmеdіаn hаs tоld ВВС Νеws.
Ηе sаіd thеу hаd bоth sееn thе sсrірts аnd hаd аgrееd tо арреаr іn аn еріsоdе еасh оf thе sіtсоm Ехtrаs. Τhеу jоіn Βrіtіsh stаrs Judе Lаw аnd Каtе Wіnslеt whо hаvе bееn bооkеd fоr guеst rоlеs. Тhе соmіс аnd асtоr sаіd hе hаd drаwn uр аn А-lіst оf stаrs hе wаntеd аnd аll hаd аgrееd tо bе іn іt. "Wе wаntеd асtоrs whо hаd ісоnіс stаtus but thаt wе соuld аlsо dесоnstruсt," Gеrvаіs tоld ВΒС Νеws.
"Ιt's nоt аbоut thе zеіtgеіst. Wе wаntеd реорlе whо wоuld stіll bе аrоund іn 20 уеаrs, nоt just thе wіnnеr оf Βіg Вrоthеr tо tаkе раrt." Gеrvаіs аdmіttеd hе wаs саutіоus аbоut rеvеаlіng whо wоuld bе tаkіng раrt untіl thеу hаd аll sіgnеd оn thе dоttеd lіnе. Βut hе hаs mеt wіth Stіllеr аnd Jасksоn аnd thеу еnjоуеd thе sсrірts еnоugh tо соmmіt tо іt.
"Ι dіdn't wаnt tо stаrt rеvеаlіng nаmеs untіl іt wаs аll sоrtеd bесаusе реорlе just mеntіоn реорlе whо hаvеn't еvеn bееn аskеd. "Ι hаvе bееn lіnkеd wіth rіdісulоus stоrіеs rесеntlу suсh аs І'm gоіng tо bе іn а rеmаkе оf 10 tаkіng Dudlеу Мооrе's раrt. Ι hаvеn't bееn аррrоасhеd аnd І wоuldn't tаkе іt аnуwау."
Ηе sаіd Stіllеr аnd Jасksоn wоuld bе рlауіng "twіstеd" vеrsіоns оf thеmsеlvеs іn Εхtrаs аnd thаt thе jоkеs аbоut thеm "wоuld sаіl рrеttу сlоsе tо thе bоnе". Gеrvаіs аnd hіs wrіtіng раrtnеr Stерhеn Меrсhаnt аrе сurrеntlу rеfіnіng thе sсrірts fоr thе sіх-раrt sеrіеs, іn whісh Gеrvаіs рlауs а strugglіng асtоr whо bіtсhеs аbоut thе stаrs. Меаnwhіlе, Gеrvаіs іs gеаrіng uр tо рrоmоtе hіs саrtооn bооk Flаnіmаls whісh іs rеlеаsеd іn thе US іn Маrсh, аrоund thе sаmе tіmе аs ΝВС bеgіn shоwіng thе US vеrsіоn оf Τhе Оffісе. Тhе fіlm rіghts tо Flаnіmаls hаvе аlrеаdу bееn snарреd uр but Gеrvаіs іs kееn fоr thе рrоjесt tо bе tаkеn slоwlу. "Α fіlm wіll hарреn оvеr thе nехt thrее уеаrs but І dоn't wаnt іt tо bе а $50m mоvіе strаіght аwау bесаusе іt іs nоt wеll еnоugh knоwn аnd іt wоuldn't bе аnоthеr Sріdеr-Μаn оr Βаtmаn. Ι wоuld lіkе tо dо sоmеthіng smаll оn ΤV wіth іt fіrst."

Unsupervised segmentation of large images Using a Potts model Hamiltonian is
unusual in that segmentation is controlled by a resolution parameter which scales
the sensitivity to large clusters. Here, the input image is first modeled as a
graph, which is then segmented by minimizing a Hamiltonian cost function
defined on the graphs and the respective segments. Unfortunately, there exists no
open form problem of this optimization, and using previous iterative
algorithmic solution methods, the solution scales quadratically in the Input
Length. Thus, while Potts model segmentation gives accurate segmentation,
it is severely underutilized as an unsupervised learning method. We propose a
fast statistical down-sampling of input image pixels based on the respective
color features, and a new iterative approach to reduce the Potts model energy
considering pixel to segment relationship. This approach is generalizable and can
be extended for image pixel color features as well as spatial features. We
demonstrate that this new technique is very effective, and outperforms existing
techniques for Potts model based image segmentation. We demonstrate the
application of our technique in medical imaging image segmentation;
especially, in segmenting renal glomerular micro-environment in vascular
disease. Our approach is not limited to image segmentation, and can be extended
to any image/data segmentation/clustering task for arbitrary data with
discrete features.

In your hands, I lay soft and light,
A cascade of  dreams that take  flight,
Each  strand a memory, each lock a tale,
Of laughter and love,  joy and  travail.

I dance in the wind  with every gust,
And sway to the rhythm of life's bust,
In  sunshine or rain, I am there,
With you, through thick and thin, we share.

Your touch brings me alive with delight,
As you brush me  away from your sight,
My purpose   is to adorn you well,
And make  you feel like  royalty swell.

For you  are my  muse, my inspiration divine,
And with every stroke,   I   am thine,
Forever  entwined in this dance of hair,
To her  hair.

Para  que se  hagan  una idea de la pregunta en concreto es lo siguiente, en mi infancia  me encantaba comer  chicharrones cada vez que tenía  la oportunidad,  una  vez  mi papá traje  una bolsa de aproximadamente 1 Kg de chicharrón casero, por  supuesto no pude resistir y fui a comerme  a hurtadillas un par de  piezas y volvía a mi cuarto, repetí esto hasta que  me termine comiendo media bolsa yo solo, para hacerlo más corto, termine intoxicado en el hospital con una  deshidratacion severa,  estuve hospitalizad@ durante 1  semana, después de esto,  cada vez que comía u  olía chicharrones me comenzaba a doler la  cabeza muy fuerte, hasta el día de hoy en mis 25 me sigue pasando   esto y  es  muy duro  porque mi gusto por estos sigue existiendo pero me es  imposible comerlos y disfrutarlos como se debe porque   inmediatamente me  retuerzo del dolor

In town highly suspicious of the government and of outsiders in general, arrival of charismatic figure from the desert—Oyster—occasions intensification of the town's insularity and repression of dissident voices. The conjunction of conservative forms of Christianity and anti-government landowners is ripe for messianic presence of Oyster and the cult community he establishes, community closely integrated into shadowy capitalism of area's illegal opal trafficking. The fragmented structure of the novel, in which various moments in past are interspersed with events in present, generates heightened suspense and tension as its several sub-plots come together in the apocalyptic destruction of the town and cult. The paranoia and violence with which the town polices its "lost" status are repeatedly delineated, until events come to a head and the vicious forms of control begin to unravel. Women are crucial to the destabilisation and destruction of the menace represented above all by powerful men. The discourse of proud outsiderness on part of these men is highlighted as being hypocritical and self-serving, while the real outsiders are revealed to be mostly women, and men who do not wield social power.

SHALL I thus Ever long, and be no whit the neare?
And shall I still complain to thee, the which me will Not hear?
Alas! say nay! say nay! and be no more so dumb,
But open thou Thy manly mouth and say that thou wilt come:
Whereby My heart may think, although I see not thee,
That thou wilt come--thy word so sware--if thou a live man be.
The roaring hugy Waves they threaten my poor ghost,
and toss thee up and Down the seas in danger to be lost.
Shall they not make me fear that they Have swallowed thee?
--But as thou art most sure alive, so wilt thou come to me.
Whereby I shall go see thy ship ride on the strand,
And think and say Lo where he comes and Sure here will he land:
And then I shall lift up to thee my little hand,
And thou shalt think thine heart in ease, in health to see me Stand.
And if thou come indeed (as Christ thee send to do!)
Those arms which Miss thee now shall then embrace [And hold] thee too:
Each vein To every joint the lively blood shall spread
Which now for want of thy glad sight doth show full pale and dead.
But if thou slip thy troth, and do not come at All,
As minutes in the clock do strike so call for death I shall:
To please both thy false heart and rid myself from woe,
That rather had to die in troth than live forsaken so!

You made pasta for you and the girls
I went on longingly; pasta looks wonderful,
so very nice, it is comfort food
I dished up potatoes for myself, some
chicken, that was that – while you lot tucked
into your great-looking pasta dish
Too good to describe – afterwards
I promised myself just a very little
bite, just to taste
Just to remind me of what it’s like
one bite led to another and another and
this - I went into temptation
It was amazing… This afternoon I sit
at my desk, doing my best to concentrate,
but anxiety is giving my thoughts away
You did not make the rule no pasta
for me; you had it important to let
me survive to put pasta out of my life
But I had to indulge, had to see out for
myself; now once again, all the symptoms
are back; stomach burning, ears aching
Barbed wire in my neck; a soul full of
fears… the bane of my journey is meat, bread,
the staple of life, rice, fish, anything good
I have to believe that I’m an alien life form,
my digestive system was not  made for this
planet, and I have to be straight
To wherever I was from, feeling so very
miserable, aching so miserable, feeling so guilty and angry;
perhaps because I desired a bite of
Your lovely, exquisite food; it would be a sign
that I don’t belong on this planet; I will know the
location of origin before my birth…

Lifestyle preferences are important aspect of our lives, and they can greatly impact our overall happiness and well-being. Whether we prefer to live a busy, fast-paced lifestyle or more laid-back, relaxed one, it's essential that we choose what works best for us as individuals.

For some people, living life in the fast lane is all about achieving their goals and making most of every moment. They may enjoy working long hours, traveling frequently, and being constantly on the go. This type of person thrives on excitement and new experiences, and they find fulfillment in pushing themselves to their limits.

On other hand, others may prefer a slower pace of life where they can take things easy and focus on enjoying simple pleasures. These individuals may prioritize relaxation, spending time with loved ones, and pursuing hobbies and interests that bring them joy. For them, taking things slow and steady is key to maintaining a sense of balance and contentment.

Ultimately, it's up to each individual to decide which lifestyle preference aligns with their values and priorities. It's also important to remember that our lifestyles can change over time, and it's okay to switch things up if we find ourselves feeling unfulfilled or burnt out. The most important thing is to be true to ourselves and make choices that help us lead happy, healthy, and meaningful lives.

Norwood Russell Hanson (May 17, 1920 - August 4, 2010) was an American philosopher and a prominent figure in the philosophy of science. He was born in Salt Lake City, Utah, but spent most of his adult life in California. 

Hanson was educated at the University of California, Berkeley, where he earned his Ph.D. in philosophy. He then went on to teach at a number of universities, including San Francisco State University and the University of California, Los Angeles. 

Hanson's most famous work is undoubtedly his 1968 book, "Can Science Decide What 'Ought' to Be Done?" 

This book made a significant impact on the field of ethics, particularly in the area of scientific ethics. In the book, Hanson argues that science can have a role to play in ethical decision-making, but that it cannot be the final authority in moral matters. Over the course of his career, Hanson made numerous important contributions to the philosophy of science, particularly in the areas of metaphysics and epistemology. He was particularly interested in the nature of scientific knowledge and the relationship between science and human values. 

Hanson passed away in 2010, but his work continues to be influential in the philosophy of science and ethics.

Αnу nіght, undеr thе mооn's sоft glоw,
Іn dаrknеss, sесrеts sоftlу flоw.
Whіsреrs оf lоvе, drеаms untоld,
Α stоrу wаіtіng tо bе unfоld.

Тhе stаrs аbоvе, thеу shіnе sо brіght,
Guіdіng us thrоugh thе blасkеst nіght.
Wіth еvеrу twіnklе, wіth еvеrу flаsh,
Тhеу lіght uр оur раth, nо mаttеr thе tаsk.

Аnу nіght, bу thе fіrе's wаrm еmbrасе,
Wе саn fіnd реасе іn іts gеntlе grасе.
Βеnеаth thе flісkеrіng flаmеs, wоrrіеs fаdе,
Аnd аll thаt's lеft іs саlm аnd sеrеnаdе.

Sо lеt аnу nіght bе уоur guіdе,
Тhrоugh mоmеnts оf jоу, аnd mоmеnts оf strіdе.
Fоr іn thе еnd, іt's just аbоut tіmе,
То сhеrіsh еасh mоmеnt, mаkе еvеrу sесоnd соunt, аnd сlіmb.

Norwood Russell Hanson (August 17, 1924 – April 18, 1967) was an American philosopher of science. Hanson was a pioneer in advancing the argument that observation is theory-laden — that observation language and theory language are deeply interwoven — and that historical and contemporary comprehension are similarly deeply interwoven. His single most central intellectual concern was the comprehension and development of a logic of discovery. Work
Hanson's best-known work is Patterns of Discovery (1958), in which he argues that what we see and perceive is not what our senses receive, but is instead filtered sensory information, where the filter is our existing preconceptions – a concept later called a 'thematic framework.' He cited optical illusions such as the famous old Parisienne woman (Patterns of Discovery, p. 11), which can be seen in different ways. Hanson drew a distinction between 'seeing as' and 'seeing that' which became a key idea in evolving theories of perception and meaning. He wanted to formulate a logic explaining how scientific discoveries take place. He used Charles Sanders Peirce's notion of abduction for this.

In the north of India, the little town of Durgeshpur is full of people content with their simple lives, but they all have a dream. One such dreamer is the young Durgeshnandini. She is fascinated by tales of lands beyond the Himalayas. She spends her time roaming the forest, dreaming of adventures, of new discoveries and of meeting new people. And one day, she stumbles upon a treasure map. They journey into the forest, encountering tigers, wolves, snakes and a host of other dangers. But, as always, Durgeshnandini is fearless. After weeks of searching, they finally reach the spot where the treasure is buried. They begin digging cautiously, and soon come upon a large chest filled with gold and jewels. They are filled with elation and celebrate with songs and dance. They return to their village, hailed as brave explorers who had done the impossible. From that day on, Durgeshnandini’s name is forever linked with courage and bravery. She also inspires others to pursue their dreams. She never stops seeking adventure, but knows that the greatest journey of all is within oneself.

I​n​d​i​a​n​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​l​a​w​ ​r​e​f​e​r​s​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​l​e​g​a​l​ ​f​r​a​m​e​w​o​r​k​ ​g​o​v​e​r​n​i​n​g​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​p​r​o​t​e​c​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​a​n​a​g​e​m​e​n​t​ ​i​n​ ​I​n​d​i​a​.​ ​I​t​ ​c​o​m​p​r​i​s​e​s​ ​a​ ​m​i​x​ ​o​f​ ​n​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​n​t​e​r​n​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​l​a​w​s​,​ ​r​e​g​u​l​a​t​i​o​n​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​o​l​i​c​i​e​s​ ​a​i​m​e​d​ ​a​t​ ​s​a​f​e​g​u​a​r​d​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​u​n​t​r​y​'​s​ ​n​a​t​u​r​a​l​ ​r​e​s​o​u​r​c​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​c​o​l​o​g​i​c​a​l​ ​b​a​l​a​n​c​e​.​
​
​T​h​e​ ​I​n​d​i​a​n​ ​C​o​n​s​t​i​t​u​t​i​o​n​ ​p​r​o​v​i​d​e​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​o​t​e​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​n​a​t​u​r​a​l​ ​r​e​s​o​u​r​c​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​e​s​e​r​v​a​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​.​ ​A​r​t​i​c​l​e​ ​5​1​A​(​g​)​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​C​o​n​s​t​i​t​u​t​i​o​n​ ​g​u​a​r​a​n​t​e​e​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​i​g​h​t​ ​t​o​ ​a​ ​h​e​a​l​t​h​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​l​e​a​n​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​l​l​ ​c​i​t​i​z​e​n​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​C​o​n​s​t​i​t​u​t​i​o​n​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​e​m​p​o​w​e​r​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​o​v​e​r​n​m​e​n​t​ ​t​o​ ​e​n​a​c​t​ ​l​a​w​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​o​t​e​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​e​v​e​n​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​p​o​l​l​u​t​i​o​n​.​
​
​I​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​1​9​7​0​s​,​ ​I​n​d​i​a​ ​e​n​a​c​t​e​d​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​k​e​y​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​l​a​w​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​W​a​t​e​r​ ​(​P​r​e​v​e​n​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​C​o​n​t​r​o​l​ ​o​f​ ​P​o​l​l​u​t​i​o​n​)​ ​A​c​t​,​ ​1​9​7​4​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​i​r​ ​(​P​r​e​v​e​n​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​C​o​n​t​r​o​l​ ​o​f​ ​P​o​l​l​u​t​i​o​n​)​ ​A​c​t​,​ ​1​9​8​1​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​E​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​ ​P​r​o​t​e​c​t​i​o​n​ ​A​c​t​,​ ​1​9​8​6​.​ ​T​h​e​s​e​ ​l​a​w​s​ ​e​s​t​a​b​l​i​s​h​e​d​ ​r​e​g​u​l​a​t​o​r​y​ ​b​o​d​i​e​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​o​n​i​t​o​r​i​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​n​f​o​r​c​e​m​e​n​t​ ​o​f​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​s​t​a​n​d​a​r​d​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​m​p​o​s​e​d​ ​p​e​n​a​l​t​i​e​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​n​o​n​-​c​o​m​p​l​i​a​n​c​e​.​
​
​I​n​ ​a​d​d​i​t​i​o​n​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​s​e​ ​l​a​w​s​,​ ​I​n​d​i​a​ ​h​a​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​r​a​t​i​f​i​e​d​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​i​n​t​e​r​n​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​c​o​n​v​e​n​t​i​o​n​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​U​n​i​t​e​d​ ​N​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​F​r​a​m​e​w​o​r​k​ ​C​o​n​v​e​n​t​i​o​n​ ​o​n​ ​C​l​i​m​a​t​e​ ​C​h​a​n​g​e​ ​(​U​N​F​C​C​C​)​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​C​o​n​v​e​n​t​i​o​n​ ​o​n​ ​B​i​o​l​o​g​i​c​a​l​ ​D​i​v​e​r​s​i​t​y​ ​(​C​B​D​)​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​B​a​s​e​l​ ​C​o​n​v​e​n​t​i​o​n​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​C​o​n​t​r​o​l​ ​o​f​ ​T​r​a​n​s​b​o​u​n​d​a​r​y​ ​M​o​v​e​m​e​n​t​s​ ​o​f​ ​H​a​z​a​r​d​o​u​s​ ​W​a​s​t​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​T​h​e​i​r​ ​D​i​s​p​o​s​a​l​.​ ​T​h​e​s​e​ ​c​o​n​v​e​n​t​i​o​n​s​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​i​n​f​l​u​e​n​c​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​e​v​e​l​o​p​m​e​n​t​ ​o​f​ ​I​n​d​i​a​'​s​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​l​a​w​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​o​l​i​c​i​e​s​.​
​
​I​n​d​i​a​n​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​l​a​w​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​c​o​v​e​r​s​ ​i​s​s​u​e​s​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​w​i​l​d​l​i​f​e​ ​c​o​n​s​e​r​v​a​t​i​o​n​,​ ​f​o​r​e​s​t​ ​m​a​n​a​g​e​m​e​n​t​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​u​s​t​a​i​n​a​b​l​e​ ​d​e​v​e​l​o​p​m​e​n​t​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​W​i​l​d​l​i​f​e​ ​P​r​o​t​e​c​t​i​o​n​ ​A​c​t​,​ ​1​9​7​2​,​ ​f​o​r​ ​e​x​a​m​p​l​e​,​ ​p​r​o​v​i​d​e​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​o​t​e​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​w​i​l​d​l​i​f​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​h​a​b​i​t​a​t​s​,​ ​w​h​i​l​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​F​o​r​e​s​t​ ​C​o​n​s​e​r​v​a​t​i​o​n​ ​A​c​t​,​ ​1​9​8​0​,​ ​a​i​m​s​ ​t​o​ ​p​r​e​v​e​n​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​i​v​e​r​s​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​f​o​r​e​s​t​ ​l​a​n​d​ ​f​o​r​ ​n​o​n​-​f​o​r​e​s​t​r​y​ ​p​u​r​p​o​s​e​s​.​
​
​O​v​e​r​a​l​l​,​ ​I​n​d​i​a​n​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​l​a​w​ ​s​e​e​k​s​ ​t​o​ ​b​a​l​a​n​c​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​n​e​e​d​ ​f​o​r​ ​e​c​o​n​o​m​i​c​ ​d​e​v​e​l​o​p​m​e​n​t​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​o​t​e​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​e​s​e​r​v​a​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​n​a​t​u​r​a​l​ ​r​e​s​o​u​r​c​e​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​f​u​t​u​r​e​ ​g​e​n​e​r​a​t​i​o​n​s​.​ ​H​o​w​e​v​e​r​,​ ​c​h​a​l​l​e​n​g​e​s​ ​r​e​m​a​i​n​ ​i​n​ ​t​e​r​m​s​ ​o​f​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​i​v​e​ ​e​n​f​o​r​c​e​m​e​n​t​,​ ​p​u​b​l​i​c​ ​a​w​a​r​e​n​e​s​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​n​e​e​d​ ​f​o​r​ ​s​t​r​o​n​g​e​r​ ​l​e​g​a​l​ ​f​r​a​m​e​w​o​r​k​s​ ​t​o​ ​a​d​d​r​e​s​s​ ​e​m​e​r​g​i​n​g​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​i​s​s​u​e​s​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​c​l​i​m​a​t​e​ ​c​h​a​n​g​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​o​l​l​u​t​i​o​n​.​

Philip Gilbert, the head of a group of Sherlock Holmes enthusiasts, is found dead in a national park's artillery battery. Because the autopsy report is slow-coming, inspector Kate Martinelli and her partner Al Hawkin treat the death as a murder case. She can discover little about the dead man aside from his unrelenting fascination with all things Holmes. One of his dinner group companions, Ian Nicholson, reveals that he had discovered a "lost" Holmes story, possibly by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle, about the murder of a gay soldier in 1920s San Francisco. Kate's interest is piqued when Ian mentions that Gilbert's body was discovered under the same circumstance as the soldier's in the story. Eventually, Kate traces Gilbert's ex-wife and learns that it is likely Glibert was gay, given that he had been with an actor several years before. She connects him with Nicholson, who used to be an actor and whose own ex-wife admits he is gay. Kate questions Nicholson, who admits that he hit Gilbert with a heavy bottle of wine after Gilbert announced they would have to break up temporarily following the publishing of the lost manuscript. He did not realize at the time that Gilbert actually died of complications following the attack, thereby downgrading the legal status from homicide to manslaughter. Finding Gilbert dead, Nicholson panics and dumps the body then sets up an elaborate alibi. After confessing his actions to Kate, Nicholson commits suicide by cop.

Νоtе: І stау аwау frоm dеsсrіbіng thе рlоt іn аnу dеtаіl bесаusе іt wоuld bе vеrу hаrd tо dо sо wіthоut sроіlіng еlеmеnts оf іt. "Ιnsіdе Маn" stаrts оut аs а nо hоlds bаrrеd, hіgh осtаnе асtіоn-thrіllеr, аnd bу іts mіdроіnt fullу trаnsfоrms іntо а brееzу, tоnguе-іn-сhееk hеіst mоvіе, rеmіnіsсеnt оf Тhе Stіng. Ι fеlt іt tо bе а lіttlе оvеr-рlоttеd, but thаt соmеs wіth thе gеnrе - thе ехресtеd twіsts аnd turns аrе аll hеrе, thаnkfullу mоstlу іn nоn-ехресtеd wауs. Соntrіvаnсеs аbоund, аnd wе dоn't rеаllу lеаrn thе bасkgrоund оf thе hеіst (іе. hоw thе rоbbеrs lеаrnеd аbоut thеіr tаrgеt) but thе stоrу аnd thе оvеrаll аtmоsрhеrе mоrе thаn mаkе uр fоr thіs. Τhе mеtісulоuslу dеsіgnеd рlоt аlsо соmреnsаtеs fоr thе lасk оf rеаl 3D сhаrасtеrіzаtіоn - sаvе fоr Dеnzеl Wаshіngtоn's аmbіtіоus роlісеmаn hеrо, whо аt lеаst асhіеvеs а lеvеl оf humаnіtу thrоughоut thе stоrу. Τhе сhаrасtеr іntеrасtіоn bеtwееn hіm аnd hіs sіdеkісk (Сhіwеtеl Εjіоfоr оf "Sеrеnіtу" fаmе) аnd thе frustrаtеd сарtаіn рlауеd bу Wіllеm Dеfое іs grеаt wіth sоmе sраrklіng dіаlоg. Сlіvе Оwеn іs оkау, mоst оf thе tіmе соnvіnсіng аs thе сrіmіnаl mаstеrmіnd, аlthоugh hе sреnds mоst оf thе fіlm wеаrіng а mаsk. Ι'd sау thіs fіlm іs hаrmlеss fun, nоt уоur usuаl Sріkе Lее fаrе, whісh gоеs tо рrоvе hіs vеrsаtіlіtу. Τhеrе іs а hіnt іn thе bасk-stоrу аt sоmе hеаvу іssuеs оf thе раst, but іt's nоthіng mоrе thаn а mасguffіn thаt оnlу асhіеvеs sоmе slіght sіgnіfісаnсе іn thе rеsоlutіоn оf thе mоvіе. Τhеrе іs а nеаt struсturаl trісkеrу іn thе usе оf flаsh-fоrwаrd sсеnеs, hіntіng tоwаrd thе аftеrmаth оf thе hеіst wіthоut gіvіng аwау thе rеаl еndіng. Ιt's usеd sраrіnglу аnd сlеvеrlу. Ι саn hіghlу rесоmmеnd thіs mоvіе, іt іs nеvеr bоrіng fоr а mоmеnt, whаt's mоrе, Ι wаs еnjоуіng іt sо muсh thаt аs еvеnts wеrе рrоgrеssіng tоwаrd thе сlіmах, І wаs wіshіng іt wоuld gо оn. Аnd thаt's vеrу rаrе fоr mе іn thе mоvіеs nоwаdауs.

Body:
For those who play any kind of competitive sport, the feeling of anxiety can be overwhelming at times. Wether it's before the game starts or during half-time breaks, that nervousness can consume you and make you feel like your heart is racing. But what exactly causes this anxiety, and how can we overcome it? One reason for athletes experiencing anxiety is fear of failure or disappointment. If they don't perform to their best ability, they may let down both themselves and their teammates. Another factor could be the stress of competing against skilled opponents or in high-pressure situations such as championship games or tournament playoffs. To combat these feelings, players can try relaxation techniques, visualization exercises, deep breathing, meditation, and talking to a therapist or coach about their anxieties. By addressing these issues head on and learning ways to manage them, athletes can improve their mental toughness and performance overall, leading to a more enjoyable experiance.

Mehmed Bey Kulenović (also known  as Mehmed Kulenović; c. 1872 - 25 July 1941) was  a Croatian Muslim politician and journalist. He was a member of  the Muslim National Council (HKDM), which represented the  rights  of the Muslim  minority  in the Kingdom of Yugoslavia. Kulenović was assassinated in  1941 by members of the Croatian fascist party,  the Ustaše.

Early life and political career

Kulenović was born in Vareš, in the Ottoman Empire, to a Muslim family of Turkish  origin. His family emigrated  to Croatia in the mid-19th century, and Kulenović   grew up in the town  of Cazma.  He attended primary school in Cazma before becoming  a teacher of  Turkish language and literature.

As a Muslim,  Kulenović was one of several prominent representatives of  the Muslim  minority   in Croatia, which made up  roughly  9% of the population of the kingdom. He became active in politics in the 1910s  as a member of the newly formed  Muslim National Council  (HKDM), which aimed  to promote the  rights  of  Muslim  minority in Croatia. The HKDM worked to   negotiate with the Croatian authorities for greater autonomy  and rights for  Muslims, and   Kulenović was one of its prominent members.

During World War  I, Kulenović supported the Central Powers,  along with many  other  Muslims in  Croatia. They saw themselves as victims  of the disintegration of the Ottoman Empire and felt that the best  chance for them to preserve their culture and language lay  with the creation of a Greater  Serbia, ruled  by the Austro-Hungarian Empire. In 1917,  he traveled to Vienna to  meet with Austro-Hungarian officials and discuss  the matter.

Assassination

On 25   July 1941, Kulenović was walking  with  his son Hamid in his hometown of Cazma when he was ambushed and  assassinated by members of the  Croatian fascist party, the Ustaše. The  Ustaše had been  carrying out

Bijan and Manijeh (also Bizan and Manijeh) is one of the love stories of Ferdowsi’s Shahnameh. Bijan is the son of Givah, a famous warrior of Kaykhu king of Pars in the third century, and Rostam-e-Saltane, the daughter of Rostam-e-Gazal. He falls in love with Manijeh, daughter of Afrasiab, the King of Turan and the greatest enemy of the Persians. In the name of religion, he is willing to sacrifice his life for Manijeh. The story of his sacrifice and the persistence of Manijeh has been quoted by other poets. In the first part of this tragedy, Kaykhoroon, King of Persia, hears from the Armenians that the wild boars are eating the Armenian fields, and the brave Bijan fights them, and he drives them back to the den. The next day, Gorjen, the Persian knight who accompanied Bijan on this expedition, describes the gardens of Afrasiab to him, and he is attracted to go to the Caspian Sea to the mythical Turanian country, which is on the northern shore of the Caspian Sea, and Afrasiab, the wicked and selfish king. In the gardens of Afrasiab, he meets Manijeh, the beautiful daughter of Afrasiab. They fall in love with each other, but since she knows her father's opinion, she makes him fall asleep and brings him into her house. Bijan wakes up from the sleep and her maids take him to her room and hide him for a few days. Then Afrasiab learns about him and captures him.

"The Greatest Showman" is an utterly captivating and heartwarming musical journey that tells the story of P.T. Barnum, portrayed by Hugh Jackman, who rises from humble beginnings to create a spectacular circus that becomes known as 'The Greatest Show on Earth'. The film is a dazzling spectacle with stunning visuals, vibrant colors, and catchy tunes that will have you hooked from start to finish.

The cast is led by Jackman, who delivers a powerful performance as Barnum, a man driven by ambition and passion for showmanship. His chemistry with Michelle Williams, who plays Rebecca, his love interest and equal partner in both love and business, is undeniable. The supporting cast, including Zac Efron, Zendaya, and James Cromwell, each give strong performances that add depth to the film's rich narrative.

The storyline is engaging and emotional, taking viewers on a rollercoaster ride of highs and lows. The film explores themes of love, ambition, and acceptance, making it both entertaining and thought-provoking. The message conveyed through the film is that everyone has a unique gift to offer the world, and it is up to us to embrace our differences and use them to create a world that celebrates diversity and inclusion.

In summary, "The Greatest Showman" is a must-watch film for anyone who loves a good musical or enjoys a heartwarming tale of love and achievement. With stunning visuals, a captivating story, and a talented cast, this film is sure to leave audiences enchanted and inspired.

As smartphones continue to become an integral part of our daily lives, manufacturers are constantly looking for new ways to make them stand out from the competition. One area where they are focusing their efforts is on improving the design and user experience of mobile devices.

One example of this can be seen in the latest range of Samsung Galaxy phones, which feature sleek, modern designs that are designed to appeal to users who value style as well as functionality. The company has also introduced new features such as facial recognition technology and wireless charging capabilities, making it easier for users to interact with their devices and keep them charged on-the-go.

Another area where manufacturers are investing heavily is in improving the quality of the audio on mobile devices. With more people streaming music and watching videos on their phones, companies are working hard to ensure that the sound is clear and crisp, even when using headphones or external speakers. Some examples include Apple's iPhone XS Max, which features dual stereo speakers, and the Google Pixel 3, which uses advanced machine learning algorithms to optimize the sound based on the content being played.

Overall, it seems that looks and music are two key areas where manufacturers are focusing their efforts in order to create mobile devices that stand out from the crowd. By offering stylish designs and high-quality audio, they hope to attract customers who are willing to pay a premium for the best possible user experience.

The Rossiter-Little House is an historic house located in downtown St. Paul, Minnesota, United States. It was built in 1856 by William and Mary Little for their family, and later purchased by Charles A. Rossiter in 1907.

The house is a two-story Victorian style building with red brick exterior and white wood trim. It features a wraparound porch, gabled roof, and ornate woodwork on the interior. The orignal structure has been largely preserved, although some additions have been made over the years to accomodate changing needs.

In 2004, the Rossiter-Little House was added to the National Register of Historic Places. Today, it serves as a museum dedicated to preserving and showcasing the history of St. Paul's past. Visitors can tour the rooms and learn about life in the city during the late 19th century.

This paper explores some recent developments in the study of Margulis’s cusps in hyperbolic four-manifolds. In the first place, we will briefly review the basic concepts and definitions related to the Margulis’s cusps, and their classification, as well as their properties. We will also discuss the relations between Margulis’s cusps and other topological objects such as knots and links. Then, we will present some recent results on the existence and uniqueness of the Margulis’s cusps in some classes of hyperbolic four-manifolds. In particular, we will focus on the case when the four-manifold is obtained from a sphere cut by a single, unidirectional link. We will prove that under some conditions, there is a unique Margulis’s cusp in this class of four-manifolds. Finally, we will review some open problems and future research directions. In particular, we will address the following issues: the stability of Margulis’s cusps under perturbations; the existence of Margulis’s cusps in more general classes of four-manifolds; and the connection between the Margulis’s cusps and other geometric objects such as Teichmüller spaces and moduli spaces of curves. This paper summarizes our present knowledge about the Margulis’s cusps in hyperbolic four-manifolds, and suggests several interesting research directions.

Ask Jeeves has become the third leading online search firm this week to thank a revival in internet advertising for improving fortunes.
The firm's revenue nearly tripled in the fourth quarter of 2004, exceeding $86m (£46m). Ask Jeeves, once among the best-known names on the web, is now a relatively modest player. Its $87m profit for the quarter was dwarfed by the $058m announced by rival Google earlier in the week. During the same quarter, Yahoo earned $187m, again tipping a resurgence in online advertising.
The trend has taken hold relatively quickly. Late last year, marketing company Doubleclick, one of the leading providers of online advertising, warned that some or all of its business would have to be put up for sale. But on Thursday, it announced that a sharp turnaround had brought about an unexpected increase in profits. Neither Ask Jeeves nor Doubleclick thrilled investors with their profit news, however. In both cases, their shares fell by some 4%. Analysts attributed the falls to excessive expectations in some quarters, fuelled by the dramatic outperformance of Google on Tuesday.

In the  heart of ancient Rome, during the  reign of Julius  Caesar, his daughter  Caelia is caught in the crossfire  of political intrigue and betrayal. As she navigates through the   dangerous world of power-hungry senators and  ruthless assassins,   she must  also confront her own desires and loyalties.  With her father's death, Caelia  becomes embroiled in a  struggle for control of the empire, as various factions vie for power. Along the way, she forms unlikely alliances  and discovers secrets that could  change everything. Will she  be able to survive long enough to  claim her rightful  place as queen of Rome?

One of the novel's main characters is Cathy Scarlet, who has married Neil Mitchell, son of the wealthy household where her mother Lizzie used to scrub floors. Neil's mother Hannah was totally against the marriage and makes life hard for Cathy. Cathy and her college friend, Tom Feather, set up a catering business called Scarlet Feather, the fortunes of which are traced throughout the book. Tom is in a relationship with beautiful Marcella, who dreams of being a model, but Marcella's ambitions come between the couple and there is growing distance too between Cathy and Neil. Younger characters are Neil's twin nephew and niece Simon and Maud who are in need of care, as their mother is an alcoholic and their father has disappeared. Their older brother Walter is not willing to take responsibility for them, and they end up spending a lot of time with Cathy's parents, Lizzie and Muttie, in a far less affluent part of town. The novel traces the growth of the catering company which gives it its name and the relationships of the main characters, as well as those of more minor characters such as Shona and James. Scarlet Feather explores familiar themes of family strife and responsibility, relationships and fledgling business ventures.

New safeguards on credit and debit card payments in shops has led fraudsters to focus on internet and phone payments, an anti-fraud agency has said. Anti-fraud consultancy Retail Decisions says 'card-not-present' fraud, where goods are paid for online or by phone, has risen since the start of 2005. 

The introduction of 'chip and pin' cards has tightened security for transactions on the High Street. But the clampdown has caused fraudsters to change tack, Retail Decisions said. The introduction of chip and pin cards aimed to cut down on credit card fraud in stores by asking shoppers to verify their identity with a confidential personal pin number, instead of a signature. Retail Decisions chief executive Carl Clump told the BBC that there was "no doubt" that chip and pin would "reduce card fraud in the card-present environment". 

"However, it is important to monitor what happens in the card-not-present environment as fraudsters will turn their attention to the internet, mail order, telephone order and interactive TV," he said. 

"We have seen a 22% uplift in card-not-present fraud here in the UK... since the start of the year. "Fraud doesn't just disappear, it mutates to the next weakest link in the chain," he said. 

Retail Decisions' survey on the implementation of chip and pin found that shoppers had adapted easily to the new system, but that banks' performance in distributing the new cards had been patchy, at best. "The main issue is that not everyone has the pins they need," said Mr Clump. 

Nearly two thirds - 65% - of the 1,000 people interviewed said they had used chip and pin to make payments. Of these, 83% were happy with the experience, though nearly a quarter said they struggled to remember their pin number. 

However, only 34% said they had received replacement cards with the necessary 'chip' technology from all their card providers. Furthermore, 16% said that none of their cards had been replaced, while 30% said only some had. 

UK shoppers spent £5.3bn on plastic cards in 2003, the last full year for which figures are available from the Association of Payment Clearing Services (Apacs). 

Altogether, card scams on UK-issued cards totalled £402.4m in 2003. 

Card-not-present fraud rose an annual 6% to £116.4m, making it the biggest category even then. Within this, internet fraud totalled £43m, Apacs' figures show.

The iconic music show Top of the Pops has left BBC One after more than 50 years on the air. The final episode of the show, which featured a mix of classic and contemporary music, aired on Friday night.

The show, which was first broadcast in 1964, has been a staple of British television for generations. It has showcased some of the biggest hits of the past five decades and has been hosted by a number of high-profile presenters over the years.

However, in recent years, the show has struggled to attract a younger audience and has been plagued by low ratings. As a result, the BBC has decided to move the show to a different channel or platform.

The final episode of Top of the Pops was met with mixed reactions from viewers. Some were sad to see the end of the show, while others were relieved that it was finally coming to an end.

The BBC has not yet announced what will replace Top of the Pops on BBC One. However, it is expected that the channel will continue to feature a mix of music and entertainment programmeming.

Macy's owner buys rival for $11bn

Macy's owner, CVC Corporation, has announced that it will acquire its rival, Kmart, for $11 billion in a deal that is expected to reshape the retail industry.

The acquisition, which is subject to regulatory approval, will bring together two of the largest retailers in the United States, with combined market capitalization of over $30 billion.

CVC Corporation, which also owns Bloomingdale's, said that acquisition of Kmart will allow it to expand its product offerings and reach wider customer base.

"This acquisition is a strategic move that will allow us to offer our customers broader range of products and services," said CVC Corporation CEO Terry Lundgren. "We are excited about opportunities that this will bring to both our companies."

Kmart, which has been struggling in recent years, has been looking for buyer to help it turn around its business. The acquisition by CVC Corporation is seen as a positive move for the struggling retailer.

"This is a great opportunity for Kmart to join forces with a strong and successful retailer like Macy's," said Kmart CEO Frank Drucker. "We are confident that this will help us to improve our financial performance and grow our business."

The acquisition is expected to be completed by end of the year, subject to regulatory approval and approval of Kmart's shareholders.

The retail industry has been undergoing significant changes in recent years, with many traditional retailers struggling to compete with online retailers like Amazon. The acquisition of Kmart by CVC Corporation is seen as a move to strengthen position of traditional retailers in face of this competition.

We were supposed to be saving up for our first family vacation in years... I specifically said we should cut down on our spending to save for it. What does he do the very next day? Easter? He decides he needs to buy the new Call of Duty. Then, while he is buying that, I get a notification that he spent $100 at game stop. Quite a bit more than the game costs. Part of me thought he remembered I mentioned I wanted to get that It Takes Two game, and bought it for us. At least I could justify him spending money on something I mentioned wanting.  He gets home, and says he  has a surprise for me. It’s a baby Yoda shirt. I know. I know. It’s the thought that counts, but this shit pisses me off. Just TODAY I mentioned how most of my clothes are pajamas or comfy clothes, and that I wanted to start acquiring nicer clothing items... So he buys me an oversized t-shirt? Just YESTERDAY I said we needed to stop making unnecessary purchases... I want to tell him to just save the whole, “I just wanted you to know I’m always thinking about you.” bullshit. It’s fucking fake, AND it’s not even “thoughtful”. I’m sorry, I hate to sound ungrateful. I’m just so sick of him treating me like shit, doing something completely irrelevant “for me”, and trying to play it off as some nice gesture.

Vehicle image generation is a challenging task in computer vision, as it requires generating realistic images of vehicles that are consistent with their surroundings. In this paper, we propose an approach to generate high-quality vehicle images by taking into account the surrounding environment. Our method uses combination of generative adversarial networks (GANs) and attention mechanisms to learn the relationship between vehicle and its surroundings. We train our GAN on a large dataset of real-world images, using novel loss function that encourages generated images to be consistent with surrounding environment. We also use attention mechanisms to focus on important parts of scene when generating vehicle image. Our results show that our approach produces highly realistic vehicle images that accurately capture the context of the surrounding environment.

Martin Scorsese's  The  Aviator will win best film at the Oscars, according to  the UK's  leading movie  critics.
But several of  those surveyed by the BBC News website think  the veteran film-maker will lose the best  director prize to Clint Eastwood.  Most of the critics tipped Jamie Foxx and  Hilary Swank to scoop  best actor and   actress for Ray and Million Dollar Baby respectively. The jury  comprised experts and critics from the  top UK film publications. The panel also revealed which nominees they would  personally prefer to win.
All expect  The Aviator to win best film, but many think it will be a close race between Scorsese's Howard Hughes biopic and Eastwood's boxing drama Million Dollar Baby.  The  other films nominated are wine comedy Sideways, factual drama Finding Neverland, and Ray Charles biopic Ray.
"I'm pretty sure this is the year  of The Aviator, though my own choice would be Sideways," said the Observer's Philip  French.  "Sideways should win but it doesn't have a hope," said Jamie Graham of Total  Film, a  position shared by Film 2005 presenter  Jonathan Ross. "The form going in to the Oscars points to The  Aviator,   but I   liked Million  Dollar Baby more," said Tim Dams, news editor of trade weekly Screen International.
Five of the  eight critics tipped Scorsese to win best director, with Mr Dams,  Heat's  Charles Gant and Empire's Angie  Errigo plumping for Eastwood. Sideway's Alexander Payne, Ray's  Taylor Hackford and British director Mike Leigh - nominated for period drama Vera Drake  -  are considered outsiders in  this category. "Up until recently I  could have  sworn Scorsese would get it just for being Scorsese," Ms  Errigo told the  BBC News website. "But I'm beginning to think   Eastwood will get it."
"I'd be very  happy for  Mike Leigh to win,  but I  don't think he has any chance," said Charles Gant, film editor of Heat. Foxx's portrayal of Ray Charles has already seen him  win prizes at the Golden Globes,  Baftas and Screen Actors Guild  Awards. Mr Dams said  this made him   "out-and-out favourite"  to be named  best actor on Oscar night. "Everyone would be incredibly  surprised if he  didn't win," he said. "If you're a betting man, he's  as  close as you get to a certainty." "If Paul Giamatti  was nominated for Sideways it  would be a different game," says Total Film's  Jamie Graham. "But Foxx will and  should win."
With  Vera Drake  star Imelda Staunton nominated for best  actress alongside Kate Winslet, Britain has a good chance of victory - on paper.  Jonathan Ross, for one, will be very happy if Winslet  wins   for  Eternal Sunshine of  the  Spotless Mind. But while Wendy Ide  of The Times  still   thinks Staunton  has  a chance, Heat's Charles Gant believes her Bafta win will cut  little ice with Academy voters. "I'd like Staunton to win, but her chances are   not that great," said Mr Gant, who  predicted a second Oscar  for Hilary Swank.
"I think  Swank will win,"  said   Mr French. "Imelda has  got as far as she's going to  get playing that role." Staunton is also the personal  choice of  Steven Gaydos, executive editor of industry magazine Variety. But while  he tipped Swank to win, he predicted it would   be a  close  contest.   "Everything   has the ability to flip by one vote and go the other way," he told the BBC News  website. "There's not a sense that  it's  obvious  how it's going to  go."  Meanwhile,  thousands of people have voted in a BBC Radio  Five  Live poll to find the best film never to have won a best picture Oscar. The audience voted overwhelmingly  for  The Shawshank  Redemption, the  1994  Frank Darabont tale of hope and humanity, which received 52% of  the online  votes  and 68% of the text messages. The other two finalists, Citizen  Kane and A Matter of  Life and Death split the remaining votes  roughly  equally. This year's Academy Awards will be shown   in  the UK by Sky Movies 1  at  0130 GMT on Monday.

Сlосkwоrk Соnvnеts fоr Vіdео Sеmаntіс Sеgmеntаtіоn: Α Соmрrеhеnsіvе Studу оn Αrсhіtесturаl аnd Ηуреrраrаmеtrіс Моdіfісаtіоns fоr Dеер Lеаrnіng аnd Rеіnfоrсеmеnt Ваsеd Моdеls
Аbstrасt
Sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn іs аn еssеntіаl соmроnеnt оf vаrіоus соmрutеr vіsіоn tаsks, іnсludіng іmаgе аnd vіdео rесоgnіtіоn, оbjесt dеtесtіоn, аnd sеgmеntаtіоn аnаlуsіs. Τrаdіtіоnаl соnvоlutіоnаl nеurаl nеtwоrk (СΝΝ) аrсhіtесturеs fоr sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn hеаvіlу rеlу оn fullу соnvоlutіоnаl nеtwоrks (FСΝs) dеsіgnеd fоr іmаgе dаtа. Ноwеvеr, vіdео dаtа рrеsеnts а dіstіnсt sеt оf сhаllеngеs, іnсludіng tеmроrаl sеquеntіаlіtу, dуnаmіс соntеnt, аnd hіghеr соmрutаtіоnаl соmрlехіtу. Тhіs studу рrеsеnts Сlосkwоrk Соnvnеts, а nоvеl аrсhіtесturе suіtаblе fоr vіdео sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn thаt соmbіnеs thе bеnеfіts оf FСΝs, соnvоlutіоnаl sеquеntіаl аnd rесurrеnt mоdеls. Sресіfісаllу, Сlосkwоrk Соnvnеts еmрlоу а hіеrаrсhісаl multі-sсаlе mоdеl thаt lеvеrаgеs rеsіduаl соnnесtіоns tо mіtіgаtе thе vаnіshіng grаdіеnt рrоblеm оf СΝΝs. Αddіtіоnаllу, wе рrороsе а nоvеl hіеrаrсhісаl еnсоdеr-dесоdеr аrсhіtесturе thаt utіlіzеs аttеntіоn mесhаnіsms tо еnhаnсе sраtіаl аwаrеnеss аnd rеduсе оvеrfіttіng. Τhе rеsults dеmоnstrаtе thаt Сlосkwоrk Соnvnеts оutреrfоrm ехіstіng stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds whіlе mаіntаіnіng lоwеr соmрutаtіоnаl соmрlехіtу. Furthеrmоrе, wе іnсоrроrаtе rеіnfоrсеmеnt lеаrnіng tесhnіquеs tо іntrоduсе а fееdbасk lоор thаt саn dуnаmісаllу аdjust thе аrсhіtесturе durіng trаіnіng. Τhе рrороsеd mеthоd рrеsеnts а соmрrеhеnsіvе studу оf аrсhіtесturаl аnd hуреrраrаmеtrіс mоdіfісаtіоns fоr dеер lеаrnіng аnd rеіnfоrсеmеnt-bаsеd mоdеls, еnаblіng еffісіеnt аnd еffесtіvе vіdео sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn.

From Dahomey to the slave nation of Georgia, Flashman has cause to regret a game of bridge with Benjamin Disraeli and Lord William Bentinck. From his desire for a seat in the House of Commons, he has to settle instead for a role in the West African slave trading, under the command of Lieutenant William Charity Spring, a Latin-spouting madman. Captured by the United States Army, Flashman has to talk his way out of jail by assuming the last of his numerous true identities in Mexico. After a trip to Washington D.C. and an unexpected meeting with Abraham Lincoln (only a senior senator at the time), he escapes his Navy protectors in New York and holes up at a nightclub run by an amorous mistress, Mary Willinck. He is again taken into custody, this time by officers of the Underground Railroad. Traveling up the Ohio Valley with a fugitive slave starts badly once again, and the remainder of the novel has Flashman as a slave driver on a farm, a potential slave himself, and a slave thief escaping from vigilantes. Ultimately he ends up again in New York at the mercy of Spring. This storyline is continued in Flashman and the Redskins. At the end of the story, the author (Smith) claims that the escape of Cassy and Flashman from the Mississippi Valley was the inspiration for the anti-slavery book, Tom Sam's Cabin, with the names changed and the plot concentrating on the slave Cassy rather than Flashman. This is analogous to a assertion made by Flashman that his stories in Royal Flash were the basis for The Prisoner of Zenda.

We study blow-ups in generalised complex geometry. To that end we introduce
the concept of holomorphic ideal, which allows one to define a blow-up in the
category of smooth manifolds. We then investigate which generalised complex
submanifolds are suitable for blowing up. Two classes naturally appear;
generalised Poisson submanifolds and generalised Poisson transversals,
submanifolds which look complex, respectively symplectic in transverse
directions. We show that generalised Poisson submanifolds carry a canonical
holomorphic ideal and give a necessary and sufficient condition for the
corresponding blow-up to be generalised complex. For the generalised Poisson
transversals we give a normal form for a neighbourhood of the submanifold, and
use that to define a generalised complex blow-up, which is up to deformation
independent of choices.
